stem: I enjoy fixing, assembling, or adjusting physical things—bikes, gadgets, models, or lab equipment—more than only talking about them.
I feel satisfied when a hands-on task has a clear “it works now” moment.
I would rather learn a skill by practicing with tools or materials than by reading theory alone.
I get pulled into researching why something happens, even when it will not be on the next test.
I like problems where I have to weigh evidence or spot what does not fit the pattern.
I trust answers more when I can trace the logic or the source myself.
How an idea looks, sounds, or feels matters to me—not only whether it is technically correct.
I like assignments where I can choose colors, layout, tone, or style—not only the final answer.
I would rather create an original poster, sketch, or short piece than fill only blank boxes on a form.
Teaching someone step-by-step feels more rewarding than finishing the same task alone in silence.
I notice when someone in a group is left out and I try to include them.
I pick group projects partly because I like coordinating how people work together.
I like pitching an idea, running a short meeting, or steering a group toward a decision.
Competitions, debates, or “sell your idea” formats energize me more than they stress me.
I often think about how to persuade others or win support for a plan.
Clear checklists, tables, or sorted folders make me feel in control of my work.
I like tasks with a standard procedure I can follow reliably each time.
Spotting a small mistake in numbers, dates, or labels bothers me until it is fixed.
